_doc: Cheat-sheet study notes for CCNA objective 1.1 — Explain the role and function of network components. Author-original summary, NOT a transcript of any copyrighted source. Schema version: cheat-sheet v2 (blueprint-aligned to 1.1.a–1.1.h).
coreConcept: 1.1 = Explain the role and function of 8 network components (routers, L2/L3 switches, NGFW+IPS, APs, controllers, endpoints, servers, PoE)

rows: Forward packets between different IP networks (Layer 3)
L2 = forward by MAC inside one LAN. L3 = also routes between LANs.
Filter by app + identity + threat sigs; IPS = inline + blocks
Bridge wireless clients to the wired LAN
Centralize control of many APs (WLC) or whole campus (DNA)
Source / destination of traffic — laptops, phones, IoT, IP cams
Endpoint that responds to client requests (web, file, DB, auth)
Power and data on one Ethernet cable. PSE supplies, PD consumes.

memoryHack: 1.1.b Switch → inside one LAN (L2 = MAC; L3 = also IP)
1.1.c NGFW = traditional firewall + app awareness + IPS
1.1.e WLC = wireless controller. DNA Center = whole-campus controller.
1.1.h PoE → power over Ethernet cable (af 15W, at 30W, bt 60-100W)

sections.title: 1.1.a — Routers
bullets: **Layer 3 device** — forwards by destination **IP address**
Connects **different networks** (LAN ↔ LAN, LAN ↔ Internet)
**Few interfaces** (2 to a handful) — boundary device, not access device
**Separates broadcast domains** — one broadcast per router interface
Each interface lives on a **different IP subnet**
Consults **routing table** for every packet → next-hop decision
Without a router (or L3 switch) one LAN cannot reach another LAN
sections.tip: Switch = many ports inside one LAN. Router = few ports across multiple LANs.
examples: Home router connects your LAN to ISP
ISR (Integrated Services Router) — Cisco enterprise router line
sections.keyLine: Router = network-to-network, forwards by IP, separates broadcast domains

sections.title: 1.1.b — Layer 2 and Layer 3 Switches
bullets: **Layer 2 switch** = forwards by **MAC address** inside one LAN
Learns MACs by watching source-MAC field → builds **MAC address table**
Unknown destination MAC = **flood out all other ports** (unknown unicast flooding)
Each port = **its own collision domain** (separates collision domains)
Default: one **broadcast domain** for the whole switch (until VLANs)
**Many ports** — 24 / 48 common
**Layer 3 switch** = L2 switch + routing engine; forwards by **MAC AND IP**
L3 switch handles **inter-VLAN routing at wire speed** (campus distribution layer)
sections.tip: L2 vs L3 switch: same chassis, L3 adds routing. L3 switch = router-on-a-switch for the campus.
examples: L2 access switch for desktop closet
sections.keyLine: L2 = MAC inside LAN. L3 = MAC inside LAN + IP between LANs.

sections.title: 1.1.c — Next-Generation Firewalls + IPS
bullets: **Traditional firewall** = packet/stateful filtering (src/dst IP, ports, protocol)
**Stateful** = tracks connection state, allows return traffic automatically
**NGFW (Next-Gen Firewall)** = traditional + 3 new powers:
① **Application awareness** — identify app by behavior, not just port (sees Facebook vs Dropbox inside HTTPS)
② **Integrated IPS** — inspects payload against attack signatures
③ **URL filter / TLS decrypt / threat-intel feeds**
**IPS = Intrusion Prevention System** — sits **inline**, can **drop** malicious packets
**IDS = Intrusion Detection System** — sits **out-of-band** (SPAN/tap), can **only alert**
Cisco NGFW family = **Firepower** (Cisco Secure Firewall). Older line = ASA.
sections.tip: Port-based rules can't see modern apps inside HTTPS. NGFW is how you actually block 'no Facebook at work.'
examples: Cisco ASA 5500-X — older non-NGFW (still on the exam)
sections.keyLine: NGFW = traditional firewall + app awareness + IPS. IPS blocks inline. IDS only alerts.

sections.title: 1.1.d — Access Points (APs)
bullets: **Bridge between wireless and wired** — radio side ↔ Ethernet side
Wireless is a **shared medium** → only one client transmits at a time per channel
Wireless is **inherently half-duplex** (collisions possible on the radio)
AP usually **PoE-powered** so only one cable runs to the ceiling
**Two AP architectures** (CCNA):
① **Autonomous AP** — self-contained, holds its own config, bridges locally
② **Lightweight AP (LWAP)** — dumb radio, paired with a WLC over **CAPWAP**
**Split-MAC architecture** (lightweight model):
- AP keeps **time-critical functions** (TX/RX, encryption, beacons)
- WLC handles **everything else** (auth, key mgmt, QoS, roaming, RF)
sections.tip: Autonomous = fine for one AP in a coffee shop. Lightweight + WLC = how every enterprise actually runs Wi-Fi.
sections.keyLine: AP = wireless↔wired bridge. Autonomous = standalone. Lightweight = WLC-managed via CAPWAP.

sections.title: 1.1.e — Controllers (WLC + Cisco DNA Center)
bullets: **WLC = Wireless LAN Controller** — manages a fleet of lightweight APs
WLC owns: SSID config, security policy, RF tuning, association, roaming
Each lightweight AP joins the WLC over a **CAPWAP tunnel**
Roaming between APs stays continuous because WLC keeps the session
WLC form factors: physical appliance (Catalyst 9800), virtual VM, embedded
**Cisco DNA Center** = **campus-wide** controller — wired switches, APs, WLCs, routers all from one place
DNA Center = **intent-based networking** → admin describes intent, DNA pushes config
DNA Center also collects telemetry → assurance + analytics + troubleshooting
sections.tip: WLC = wireless-only. DNA Center = wired + wireless + everything. Both are 'controllers' on the blueprint.
examples: Cisco DNA Center appliance for whole-campus SDN
sections.keyLine: WLC controls APs. DNA Center controls the whole campus.

sections.title: 1.1.f — Endpoints
bullets: **Endpoint = source or final destination** of traffic
Lives at the **edges** of the network, not in the middle
Examples: laptops, desktops, phones, **IP cameras, IoT sensors, printers**
Every endpoint has a **NIC (Network Interface Card)** — wired (RJ45/fiber) or wireless (radio)
Endpoint ≠ network device. Switches/routers/firewalls move traffic; endpoints generate or consume it.
Servers are endpoints (just ones that respond)
sections.tip: If a question asks 'which device joins/links/forwards' — endpoint is wrong. Endpoints are what get connected.
examples: Laptop, iPhone, IP camera, smart thermostat, conference-room display
sections.keyLine: Endpoint = traffic source/destination at the network edge

sections.title: 1.1.g — Servers
bullets: **Server = endpoint that responds** to client requests
Role, **not hardware**: same box can be client one moment, server the next
Common server services: **HTTP/HTTPS (web), SMB/NFS (file), DB, mail, DNS, time, auth**
Servers usually serve **many clients at once**
On the CCNA: server is listed as a 1.1 component because the exam wants you to recognize 'server' as an endpoint role, not a category of network gear
sections.tip: Client requests, server responds. The label is about direction of the request, not make/model.
sections.keyLine: Server = endpoint that responds. Role, not hardware.

sections.title: 1.1.h — PoE (Power over Ethernet)
bullets: **Power + data on the same Ethernet cable** → no separate power outlet for ceiling devices
**PSE = Power Sourcing Equipment** (the switch supplying power)
**PD = Powered Device** (the AP, IP phone, IP camera being powered)
PSE and PD **negotiate** during link bring-up → PSE delivers only what PD needs
Switch tracks **total PoE budget** across all ports — a 370 W budget can't fully run 24 PoE+ devices at max draw
**Standards (memorize):**
- **802.3af 'PoE'** — up to **15.4 W** at PSE (~12.95 W at PD)
- **802.3at 'PoE+'** — up to **30 W** at PSE (~25.5 W at PD)
- **802.3bt 'PoE++'** — up to **60 W (Type 3)** or **100 W (Type 4)** using all 4 pairs
**Mode A** = power on data pairs (1/2 + 3/6). **Mode B** = power on spare pairs (4/5 + 7/8). 802.3bt uses both.
sections.tip: Common trap: switch has a 'PoE+' port but the total PoE budget caps how many ports can run at full draw simultaneously.
examples: Lightweight AP, IP phone, IP camera, IoT door lock — all PDs
Cisco Catalyst 9300 with 1100 W PoE budget
sections.keyLine: PoE = power + data on one cable. af=15W, at=30W, bt=60-100W. PSE supplies, PD consumes, switch budgets total.

wrong: Router ≠ connect many PCs (that's a switch)
L2 switch ≠ reach the internet alone (need router or L3 switch)
IDS ≠ block traffic (only alerts; IPS blocks)
Autonomous AP ≠ uses WLC (only lightweight APs do)
right: Router = forwards between networks by IP
L2 switch = forwards inside LAN by MAC
NGFW = traditional firewall + app awareness + IPS
IPS = inline, drops. IDS = out-of-band, alerts.
Lightweight AP + WLC over CAPWAP = enterprise wireless

quickAnswerMap: Connect many PCs in one office
Drop a known attack signature inline
Alert on suspicious traffic, no blocking
Bridge wireless clients to the wired network
Manage entire campus (wired + wireless) by intent
Power an AP through its Ethernet cable
oneLineSummary: 1.1 covers 8 components: routers (L3), L2/L3 switches (LAN), NGFW+IPS (security), APs (wireless bridge), controllers WLC+DNA (centralized mgmt), endpoints (edges), servers (responders), and PoE (power+data on one cable).
sourceLabel: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 1 — Network Devices (expanded to full 1.1 blueprint)
